sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Vision changes and difficulty reading, particularly in dim light.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports difficulty adjusting to dim lighting, starting approximately three to four months ago. He notes that straight lines, such as those in his door frame, appear crooked. He and his wife have been attending fancy restaurants recently, and he experienced similar difficulty at an Italian restaurant the previous night. He has tried hypnosis, lozenges, and chewing gum to quit smoking, but has been unsuccessful. He reports smoking approximately one pack per day and has a history of hypertension, for which he has not been consistently taking medication.
Findings

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
The otoscopic examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes. The right eye cornea is clear with no visible micro-villar inclusions and the disc-to-cup ratio is 0.2. The fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina attached at 360 degrees with no holes or tears and RPE changes in the fovea. Subretinal fluid and hemorrhage are present. The left eye cornea is clear with no micro-villar inclusions and the disc-to-cup ratio is 0.3. RPE changes are present in the fovea, but no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid is noted. Diagnostic testing reveals drusen deposits under the RPE in the right eye and subretinal fluid accumulation and choroidal neovascular membrane visualization in the right eye.
Assessment
The diagnostic testing and eye examinations are consistent with neurovascular age-related macular degeneration, specifically wet macular degeneration, which is advanced to a stage that typically leads to more significant vision loss.
Plan

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: I will initiate monthly intravitreal injections of lucentol in the vitreous of both eyes after numbing the eye to minimize discomfort. These injections aim to slow the progression of vision loss associated with wet macular degeneration. I will schedule the first injection today and will follow up in four weeks to assess response and adjust the treatment plan as needed. The nurse will prepare the patient for the injection, and I will return in a few minutes.
